The tone of your comment would indicate that it would be fruitless to try to explain.  However, start with this:  dilbit is NOT crude oil.  It's not even "heavy oil" as Wente says.  It's diluted bitumen (tar).  When (not if) dilbit spills on water, the diluent evaporates quite quickly and the remaining bitumen sinks to the bottom.  There is currently no known technology to recover and clean up after such bitumen without destroying the ocean floor or riverbed in the process.  Kalamazoo.

The argument that Kinder Morgan has been pumping crude oil through the existing pipeline for years without any problems is like saying we have been playing Russian roulette with only one chamber loaded and since nothing has happened so far, let's load two chambers and see what happens.

Dilbit is a dinosaur product with no effective disaster recovery plan.

The legalities of BC's position are another matter.

Okay, this is a  'relatively speaking' thing. Compared to guns - yes, the Zulu's weapons were inferior. But compared to the weaponry in use in Southern Africa by the other tribes at the time, it was vastly superior. 
Early-to-mid 19th century Southern African weapons of war were largely confined to the throwing spear. A ranged weapon that offered a good deal of safety. Coupled with light leather shields - designed to deflect an incoming spear (it couldn't have blocked anything), and a club (knopkierie) in the event that somebody did get in close. 
Now compare that to the Zulu Assegai that Shaka designed. That weapon is useless, in fact almost impossible, to throw. It has a long, broad blade (far longer the handle). That's not a spear - even if he called it one. It's a broad-sword. Shaka brought swordsmanship to Southern-Africa. He couples this with a much larger shield, designed to also provide close-range defense.

An unstoppable infantry in a land of light artillery.

In the West this low barrier became the communion rail; and rather strangely, the ambo was moved from the centre of the nave and became TWO pulpit-like structures on the left and right side of the low barrier. The left "ambo" was reserved for the Gospel reading, and the right "ambo" was used for the other readings and also for the psalmody surrounding the readings. Thus emerged the "Gospel side" and "Epistle side" of the sanctuary in the Tridentine Liturgy.

In the East, the low barrier became the Iconostasis. The first development seems to have been the erection of a wooden superstructure of pillars and beams on top of the low barrier. This superstructure was NOT opaque, and did not provide any sort of visual barrier for the people following the Eucharistic action. Its purpose was strictly decorative. However, once that superstructure was in place, the temptation to hang moveable icons on the superstructure proved irresistible. With time, it became more solid and multi-layered.

Pepsi-Cola is not the same as Coca-Cola, as the trademark office determined when they let both be trademarked. Has double cheeseburger been trademarked by McDonald's? Doubtful. The trademark and patents office doesn't allow trademarks on everything.

The BLM published an EIS in 2008 that did sophisticated projection of the forest under a wide range of management strategies specifically  for the O&C  forest.  Included in this range of alternatives were harvest levels at and over 500 million board feet.  Under all of the management strategies considered there was an increasing level of carbon from today's conditions.   The total error associated with these estimations, although it cannot be precisely quantified, is greater than the difference among the alternatives.   Annual harvest is a tiny fraction of the overall carbon equation especially with the long cycle between harvest the BLM contemplated at the time.  Increasing carbon stores and increasing harvest, over the modest levels practiced under the NWFP, are certainly possible.  This is not an either or choice.

Why not just emulate the ETF and buy all the banks?

Rob C and I discussed this about a year ago, with this same ETF. 

At $9.95 per trade, if you have $9,630 or more to invest in banks you will save money buying equal amounts of each bank in your brokerage account.  Then for an additional $59.70 a year you could rebalance annually. This saves money on fees AND you will earn a greater yield. 

At least one of the big 5 offers a $6.95 trade (CIBC), and if you deal with them the point at which just doing it yourself makes sense is just over $6700. 

Personally, I doubt I would bother to rebalance annually. I would save the fees and only rebalance if things got really out of balance. 

I personally hold 4 of the big 5 (I don't recognize National as a big bank) and intend to add the 5th (BMO) at some point in the future. 

I think if you don't have enough $ to hold all, either go with the highest yielding (CM) or one of the two biggest (RY,TD). Either way, I think you will be fine.

Teaching children programming using a well designed, interactive language can give kids a basic, intuitive understanding of algebra years before they ever open an algebra text book. Teach a child coding and they gain an intuitive understanding of concepts like functions, variables, recursion, etc., and and they will develop critical thinking skills that allow them to break down problems in to constituent parts and solve them in a sensible order. In short, they will gain familiarity with mathematical processes. Sure, some of them might develop a liking for it, just as some kids develop a liking for history, but that's missing the point. The point is not to create new 'coders,' the point is to give them a leg up when they start learning algebra, so when they are introduced to new concepts they can say "oh, hey I've seen something like this before."

Coding is the one of the best tools we've ever come up with for early math education. As always, the effectiveness depends on the teacher.

Let me tell you exactly what you're up against.

I am very interested in the comment by Trudeau that, "“We have to move beyond the Indian Act, we have to end the Indian Act but we can’t do it with the stroke of the pen from Ottawa..."  I understand Ottawa tried before, in 1998, with Bill C-49.  

C-49 took Natives OUT of the Indian Act, in that their community leaders (chiefs, council members) would act on bands' behalf in matters regarding reserve lands and resources, replacing federal administrators. One stipulation: band members had to understand the change and agree to it.  In B.C., Squamish members were stunned to learn their leaders had already signed the agreement--which they knew nothing about.  The public was  stunned to learn Squamish dissidents said "No" to the concept; it would have given chiefs and council members even MORE power than they already had abused. The House passed it anyway.

The addition of this new public transportation option to the central city -- an option that requires no tax input for operations whatsoever -- is likely to be a net positive for road maintenance.

Tactics of Rebuttal: The 4D Approach

Russia’s narrative can be viewed as an offensive weapon.  When it comes to defending Russia, different tactics are used. They can be summed up in four words: dismiss, distort, distract, dismay.

Dismiss: The first Russian approach to negative reporting or comment is to dismiss it, either by denying the allegations on the ground, or denigrating the one who makes them.

Distort: Russian officials and media have also developed a strong tendency to distort information to serve their overall narrative.

Distract: A further tool in Russia’s information arsenal is distraction: turning attention away from the activities of Russia and its allies by launching accusations elsewhere.

Dismay: The final key tool in Russia’s communications arsenal is to spread dismay, by warning that moves which Russia opposes will have disastrous consequences for those planning them.

It’s a brand of information warfare, known as dezinformatsiya.

The matter of Rail begins with discerning when to look at the forest and when to instead look at the trees.  Initially, A project begins at the forest view and future vision.  So, the burdens fall upon the affirmative actor, i.e. the project proposers.  A future vision does not meet such a burden. The "top 20%" (1 person) pays over 99% of the taxes.

Instead, it begs the question "how can the top be paying so much of overall taxes while paying a lower RATE than workers?

This is a pretty poorly written article, as it starts out with "myths" then goes on to mix various principles in federal and state laws and court cases without explanation.  

This statement is just plain wrong:
"[S]exual harassment cases rest on basis of prima facie and a preponderance of the evidence. Prima facie means "what would a reasonable person conclude?" In other words, what would 12 reasonable people conclude when looking at all the facts?"

There is no such thing as "prima facie."  There is something called a "prima facie case."  That is a list of principles that a party in a lawsuit must prove in order to win their case; each claim in a party's case has a different list of principles.  Many lists of principles are not in statutes but are the result of multiple court decisions.  Some principles require more proof (evidence) than mere preponderance.

A prima facie case of sexual harassment depends on state law, federal law, and court cases.

I have been to Deline in summer twice. The first was the end of a five week solo canoe trip from Behchoko on Great Slave Lake. Fortunately the lake is full of fish. The water is clear. There is no pollution in it and the natives want to keep it that way. Not like Lake Athabasca downstream from the oil sands operation. Traffic on the road in town is mostly four wheelers (usually  carrying passengers) and pedestrians. Trucks are few. I watched hand games in the community center. A guy from one team passes a small object from hand to hand and the opposing team guesses which hand it is in. And the rest of the first team bangs on drums and yells at the top of their voices. The noise is deafening. The town is dry, at least during the tourist season. Surely a good place to end a canoe trip.

Of course defence lawyers deliberately delay proceedings.  Anyone who thinks otherwise is naive.  Delay is defence counsels' strongest ally.  Witnesses move away, die, can't be located, change their mind about testifying or otherwise become unavailable.  Those who remain are subject to the same phenomenon as all of us:  with the passage of time memories fade, become less precise, less certain and therefore more vulnerable on cross-examination.

That said, is deliberate delay actually an improper defence tactic?  After all, it merely plays on an evidentiary characteristic of our adversarial trial system:  the reliability or vulnerability of witnesses.

What the SCC seems to be saying here, in effect, is that some amount of deliberate delay is permissible, that trial judges have the ability to control it through their interlocutory orders, and that if  counsel still refuse to rein it in, then the trial judge can penalize them personally with an award of costs.  What's the problem?

Lets take an example. California has a system of partial direct democracy where any citizens can propose a potential law. If enough people sign on, then a referendum is held, and if a majority votes yes - then government is required to implement that law.  These are known as "ballot initiatives". Last year California voted on a ballot initiative to set aside 1.2 billion dollars to build housing for the homeless and address it's massive homelessness problem. The funding would come from an increase in property taxes to be set aside exclusively for that fund.

Would people vote to increase their own taxes to help those less fortunate ? Yes. The initiative passed by an overwhelming majority - one of the largest ever. California's home-owners were happy to pay more in taxes IF they could dictate that the money goes to shelter for the homeless.

The article uses $300,000 for a Brantford house and $ 770,000 for a Toronto house. Amortized over 25 years, the difference is $18,800 per year. The cost of transportation (here he uses VIA and TTC) and/or of a car also increase that price.  More important than its price, what is its value? Non-monetary factors such as personal time, family time, social time, stress and health,  carbon footprint, city planning etc. have value. If the job is so good, move closer to it and pay more for accommodation or if you have to, rent to decrease costs. If the Brantford house is so good, get a job closer to home. 
These types of personal decisions would help direct collective decision regarding intensification, sprawl and transportation. Public policy should build in incentives to nudge these types of personal decisions.
